
## Note for the author (FHI)
1. The "Attention Dilution" Problem
LLMs have a finite amount of "attention" (weights) they can apply to a prompt.
If you give one agent 50 instructions—"Check for logs, check for JsonNode deep copies, 
remember the roadmap, follow the philosophy, be pedantic but also helpful"—the model starts 
to "blur" the requirements. It effectively averages your instructions, leading to the 
"Mystery Errors" and "Lax" enforcement you want to avoid.

By splitting them, you ensure 100% of the Critic’s "cognitive energy" is focused on hunting 
for violations, while 100% of the Senior’s energy is focused on high-level synthesis.

2.Overcoming "Politeness Bias"
LLMs are RLHF-trained (Reinforced Learning from Human Feedback) to be helpful and agreeable. 
A single "sensible" agent will often see a mistake, think "Well, I see what they were trying 
to do," and let it slide to be "helpful."

The Critic Persona is a "hack" to bypass this politeness. By telling it to be "Brutal" and 
"Pedantic," you force the model into a different mathematical state where it is rewarded 
for finding faults rather than being agreeable.
